reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child welfare (predictability, safety, reduced test pressure on age 10), school choice accessibility, commuting feasibility, equity of opportunity for my child regardless of family SES.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children (6–12 years old), across SES bands, in both urban and rural settlements; those for whom school choice is constrained by commute distance, information access, or cost of test preparation.
reactions.public_good_frame: A primary school system that sorts on demonstrated ability rather than family wealth, preserves childhood time for play and development, and does not penalise rural or low-SES families for transport or coaching costs.
reactions.argument: I support reducing test-prep pressure on 10-year-olds and reweighting admission to reduce the coaching-arms-race — my child's wellbeing depends on not needing private tutoring to access a safe, good school. However, this scenario relies on three unproven mechanisms: (1) a non-gameable SES proxy that does not yet exist and Hungary has no register to build from; (2) a coaching-resistant assessment that has not been demonstrated; (3) the assumption that low-SES families underapply only due to information gaps, when transport and prior exposure are also barriers. If the quota depends on a proxy that parents and schools can game, or if the assessment is redesigned but still rewards coaching, or if outreach fails because the barriers are structural (commute, childcare during long school days), then the reform delivers no gain to my child and adds complexity and cost. My support is conditional on: (a) transparent pilot data showing the SES proxy resists gaming and is built from real administrative registers; (b) psychometric evidence that the new assessment scores do not correlate with test-prep spending; (c) qualitative evidence from under-applying settlements identifying whether barriers are remediable by outreach.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would shift to oppose if: (i) the SES indicator is constructed from parental declaration or school estimates and is shown to be gameable in pilot; (ii) psychometric analysis reveals the new assessment still rewards coaching (e.g., high correlation with prior tutoring spend); (iii) outreach campaigns in under-applying settlements show <10% increase in applications after two cohorts, indicating barriers are structural not informational. I would strengthen support if: a multi-year pilot demonstrates non-gameability of the SES proxy, coaching-resistance of the assessment, and >30% application increase in at least three under-applying rural settlements.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: School choice accessibility, child safety and familiarity with school environment, predictability of pathway, equity of opportunity to remain with peers.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children who rely on public selective schools as a mobility or stability asset; parents in rural or low-SES settlements where school choice is constrained; middle-class parents who view selective tracks as insurance against general-school underfunding.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system that preserves accessible pathways for capable children regardless of SES, does not create a second tier of degraded general schools as selective seats shrink, and does not force parents to exit public education in response to shrinking opportunity.
reactions.argument: Phasing down selective entry over a decade sounds gradual, but it directly threatens my child's opportunity if I have limited choice. The scenario acknowledges its own fatal assumption: that non-state (church and foundation) maintainers will not offset state-sector caps. They will. In Hungary, private and church schools already absorb selective demand in urban areas; phasing state selective seats will accelerate that exit, not reduce sorting. The residual selective places in state schools become more SES-skewed (fewer seats, same high-SES applicant pool), while non-state schools absorb the sorted demand and relocate it beyond public oversight. Meanwhile, general schools do not improve — the peer-effect benefit assumes high-achievers stay in general schools, but if places shrink without admission reform, high-SES families exit instead. The cost of parallel administration while grandfathered cohorts run dual systems for a decade is recurring, not transitional, and that cost comes from schools I use. I also face a legal and practical gamble: the scenario cites prior curtailment attempts (1990s–2010s) triggering sustained opposition. My child's schooling should not depend on whether this government survives two electoral cycles.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would conditionally support S2 only if paired with: (i) legally binding and monitored caps on non-state selective intake (not merely negotiated); (ii) simultaneous S1-type admission reform so residual selective places are reweighted toward low-SES pupils; (iii) guaranteed expansion and funding of general-school upper grades so they are demonstrably competitive with retained selective programs; (iv) multi-year transition costs fully funded without budget cuts to my child's school. I would shift to support if evidence from a five-year pilot in 3–5 school networks showed that capped state selective seats, combined with binding non-state intake rules and peer-composition funding for general schools, did not trigger private-school exit and did not degrade general-school attainment relative to baseline. Without binding non-state caps and parallel admission reform, I oppose because the risk of sorting-migration is moderate and the downside for my child is high.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing (no early sorting, childhood preserved), school choice accessibility, peer diversity and social cohesion, safety and long-term stability of school pathway, equity of opportunity regardless of SES.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children across SES bands, especially in rural and low-SES urban settlements where school choice is limited; parents who value peer diversity and do not wish their child locked into a lower track at age 10; parents concerned about the social and psychological effects of early sorting.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system that defers judgment of children until age 14, preserves comprehensive peer groups through early adolescence, widens access to high-achieving peers for all children, and does not reproduce parental SES as school placement.
reactions.argument: S3 addresses the root cause that other scenarios leave intact: the sorting mechanism itself. If early selection concentrates low-SES pupils in general schools before academics stabilise, deferring selection to age 14 gives my child and others time to show capability without parental wealth filtering the verdict. International evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) shows no mean achievement loss from late selection; the peer-effect channel is contested but the cross-country association is moderate. However, the scenario contains an unresolved tension: it assumes sorting does not simply migrate to school choice and non-state maintainers, yet the equity-impact field calls this migration 'the largest risk.' Poland reversed a similar reform after two cycles. Hungary currently lacks the teacher quality and diagnostic infrastructure that made Finland's transition work. My support depends on three conditions: (1) binding intake rules for church/foundation schools preventing sorting migration; (2) genuine commitment to teacher retraining and diagnostic support, not just legislative change; (3) political stability beyond the next election. If any of these fails, comprehensive schools become warehouses for low-SES pupils while middle-class families exit to private schools, and I have no recourse for my child.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would move from conditional to strong support if: (i) pre-implementation data from 2–3 school networks piloting comprehensive intake show non-state maintainers do not expand selective capacity or private enrollment does not increase >5% when public selective seats shrink; (ii) teacher-retraining timelines are binding, with teacher-supply targets and accountability, not advisory; (iii) at least two major church/foundation maintainers publicly commit to binding intake rules with enforcement audits. I would move to oppose if: (a) binding intake rules are not legislated or are legislated but have no enforcement mechanism; (b) teacher shortages worsen before retraining begins (i.e., implementation is deferred indefinitely); (c) a single electoral cycle shows >15% increase in private-school enrollment in urban areas, indicating sorting migration. I would also oppose if costs exceed credible budget commitments within three years of rollout.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child welfare (no early sorting, equitable peer access), school choice accessibility, long-term stability and equity of opportunity regardless of SES.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children in general schools and low-SES/rural settlements who have no school choice; parents concerned that compensatory funding will not offset the effects of early selection; parents who value peer diversity across SES within a school.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system that does not sort children at age 10 and then attempt to repair the damage downstream; targeted funding should accompany structural equity, not substitute for it.
reactions.argument: S4 is the path of least institutional disruption and maximum political durability for policymakers, but minimal protection for my child. It retains the sorting mechanism — age-10 selection concentrating low-SES pupils in general schools — and proposes to 'compensate' via TEIP-style funding, rigorous standards, and clustering. The problem is twofold: (1) No country has demonstrated that downstream compensation offsets early sorting at Hungary's selection age (10–12). Portugal's compensatory package did not select as early, and Portugal did not alter selection age — they just funded general schools better. This is not proof of offset at Hungary's timing. (2) Targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation. Hungary's education budget is already fragile; without structural reform, compensatory funding is a political commitment with no legal protection. My child's school could be well-funded one year and underfunded the next depending on the electoral cycle and fiscal policy. By contrast, S3 (structural reform) creates a durable change to the system itself — no government can cheaply undo comprehensive schooling without visible reversal. S4 leaves the sorting engine intact and relies on funding that is politically vulnerable and unproven at Hungary's timing. The scenario acknowledges that compensation may entrench rather than dismantle sorting — if governments can claim they have 'solved' equity via funding, they remove pressure to address the root cause.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would conditionally support S4 only if paired with: (i) constitutional or multi-year-binding budget protections guaranteeing that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is protected from consolidation cuts (i.e., locked-in revenue source, not annual discretionary allocation); (ii) legislative guarantee that selection age cannot revert without parliamentary supermajority vote; (iii) independent, pre-implementation comparison-group evidence from at least one comparable country (Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland) showing that compensation at Hungary's selection age reduces the SES-outcome gradient by >10 percentage points within five years. Without all three, I oppose because the downside is that my child remains sorted at age 10, funding evaporates in a budget crisis, and the system's equity problem is institutionalised as 'compensable' rather than 'fixable.' I would shift to conditional support only if credible evidence from a real pilot (not modelling) in 3–5 disadvantaged Hungarian school networks shows that targeted funding combined with curriculum standards actually reduces the SES-outcome gradient when selection age is unchanged.
